---
title: "4. Preserve & Share"
description: "Archive research outputs, publish openly, obtain persistent identifiers, and maximize research impact"
phase_order: 4
sidebar: open-research-cycle
number-depth: 4
number-sections: true
number-offset: [0,4,0]
page-layout: full
css: orc-styles.css
title-block-style: none
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.0/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
---

<div class="orc-phase-hero-compact">
<div class="orc-hero-cycle phase-4">
{{< include ../images/os-cycle.svg >}}
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-body">
<div class="orc-hero-tagline">Make your research outputs accessible, reusable, and citable</div>
<div class="orc-hero-pills">
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Data Sharing</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Code Publishing</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Open Access</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Persistent Identifiers</span>
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-checkpoint"><i class="bi bi-people-fill"></i> Checkpoint: Manuscript submission with DOIs for preregistration, data, and code</div>
</div>
</div>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary markdown="1"><i class="bi bi-database-check"></i> 

### Data Sharing {#sec-data-sharing}

</summary>

Sharing your data allows others to verify your findings, build on your work, and increase the impact of your research. But sharing does not always mean making everything publicly available. What you can share depends on the consent you obtained, the sensitivity of your data, and your ethics approval.

In [1. Plan & Design](01_plan_and_design.qmd){.mark}, you wrote a Data Management Plan, in [2. Collect & Manage](02_collect_and_manage.qmd){.mark}, you created documentation for yourself, and in [3. Analyze & Collaborate](03_analyze_and_collaborate.qmd){.mark} you updated that documentation for your collaborators. Now you prepare that documentation for people who have no prior knowledge of your project. You also reassess your plan and make a **final decision on how openly your data will be shared, select an appropriate repository, and apply a license that tells others how they may reuse your work**.

This step is straightforward if you followed previous steps: by this stage, you should already have a README, a data dictionary (codebook), organized files, and clear metadata.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### 4.1.1. Open vs Restricted <!-- I edited this a bit Reema, is this correct? overall I think this section could use more information-->

Not all data can or should be shared openly. Your sharing options depend on what consent participants gave and what your ethics approval permits (see [1.2.3. Ethics](<!--internal link-->)).

<br>

- **Open data** can be downloaded by anyone without restrictions. This maximizes reuse but is only appropriate when data contains no personal information or has been fully anonymized.

- **Restricted access** means the metadata is available on a professional repository and clear instructions of the conditions of access to the data are provided. Requesting the data often requires describing the purpose of reuse (sometimes in the form of a preregistration, see [1.4.1. Pre-analysis planning](<!--internal link-->)), signing a Data Use Agreement, and agree to further conditions. This can be organized automatically through the repository or through contacting the authors<!--if so what's the difference with the option below?-->. This balances reuse with protection. 

- **Metadata only** describes what data exists without sharing the data itself. Others can discover your work and contact you to discuss access. This is appropriate when data cannot be shared due to legal or ethical constraints.<!--would that be considered Accessible though?--> Making your data Findable by sharing your metadata on a professional repository is the first step to making your data FAIR as required by the [LMU guidelines](https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/media/contenthub/amtliche-veroeffentlichungen/gwp-ordnung.pdf) (see [1.2.1. LMU guidelines](<!--internal link-->))

<br>

When deciding, ask yourself: What did participants consent to? What does your ethics approval allow? Can the data be anonymized without losing value (see [2.3.2. Anonymisation](<!--internal link-->))? If you work with sensitive data, consult [your institution's data protection officer](https://www.lmu.de/en/about-lmu/structure/organizational-structure/officers-and-representatives/data-protection-officer.html).

<!-- TODO Reema: tool card for decision tree ; MI: not sure if feasible in enough details to be useful? but perhaps ;)--> 


::: {.container .my-4 }

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Lecture</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Why share data openly?</h4>
<p class="card-text">An introduction to the what, why, and how to make data open (30 min)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/training/data-management/open-data.html" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Lecture</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Maintaining Privacy with Open Data</h4>
<p class="card-text">How to make data open without revealing sensitive information (1h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/training/data-management/maintaining-privacy-with-open-data.html" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::


::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial (tba) </span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Data Anonymization</h4>
<p class="card-text">Implement data anonymization techniques in R. (X h)</p>
<!-- Add link when available -->
<a href="#" class="stretched-link" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

<h4 class="fs-5 mt-3"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool w-100 h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon mb-2">
<img src="https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/assets/img/favicon.ico" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU logo">
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">LMU Guidelines for Safeguarding Good Scientific Practice</h4>
<p class="card-text">Implementation of the German Research Foundation's (DFG) Code of Conduct</p>
<a href="https://cms-cdn.lmu.de/media/contenthub/amtliche-veroeffentlichungen/gwp-ordnung.pdf" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>

:::

:::

:::




#### 4.1.2. Preparing Your Data

During the course of data collection and analyses, you created a README and data dictionary for yourself and collaborators. Before sharing publicly, review them from the perspective of someone who knows nothing about your project.

<br>

- **Expand your README** to include the research context (who created the data, what it contains, when and where it was collected, why it was generated, how it was produced), how to cite the dataset, and any access conditions. 

- **Review your data dictionary** to ensure every variable is fully described. What was obvious to you during analysis may need explanation for others.

- **Confirm you follow community standards.** Many fields have established formats for sharing data (e.g., [BIDS](https://bids.neuroimaging.io/index.html) for neuroimaging, see [FAIRsharing](https://fairsharing.org/) and [RDMkit](https://rdmkit.elixir-europe.org/your_domain) to review discipline specific organizational and metadata standards). Using these makes your data immediately usable with existing tools (see also [2.2.5. Standards](<!--internal link-->)). 

- **Verify anonymization.** Check that no personal information remains in the data files, metadata, or filenames.


::: {.container .my-4 }

<h4 class="fs-5"><i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE</h4>

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}

<!-- card  -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">FAIR Data Management</h4> 
<p class="card-text">Manage data following FAIR principles. Covers READMEs, data dictionaries, file formats, and standards. (2h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/data-documentation-validation-R/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::

<!-- card  -->
::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Data Documentation & Validation</h4> 
<p class="card-text">Create data dictionaries and READMEs for your data. (1h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/data-documentation-validation-R/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::


::: {.col}
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<span class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</span>
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" class="img-fluid" alt="LMU OSC logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">Data Anonymization (tba)</h4> 
<p class="card-text">R anonymization techniques for safely sharing sensitive research data.</p>
<a href="#" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::

:::


<h4 class="fs-5 mt-3"><i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES</h4>
<!-- row for Tools & Resources section -->

::: {.row .row-cols-1 .row-cols-md-2 .row-cols-lg-3 .g-4}


::: {.col}
<!-- Actual card component -->
<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/29867987?s=280&v=4" class="img-fluid" alt="FAIRsharing logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">FAIRsharing</h4>
<p class="card-text">Search by discipline to find metadata standards, reporting guidelines, and data policies for your field.</p>
<a href="https://fairsharing.org/" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"> </a>
</div>

:::

::: {.col}

<div class="card orc-card-tool h-100 position-relative">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icons mb-2">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon icon-1">
<img src="https://rdmkit.elixir-europe.org/assets/img/apple-touch-icon.png" class="img-fluid" alt="RDMkit logo">
</div>
</div>
<h4 class="card-title">RDMkit</h4>
<p class="card-text">Domain-specific metadata standards</p>
<a href="https://rdmkit.elixir-europe.org/your_domain" class="stretched-link" target="_blank" rel="noopener"></a>
</div>
:::

:::

:::


#### 4.1.3. Where to Deposit

Choose a repository that suits your data type, your field's expectations, and your access requirements.

**Discipline-specific repositories** are often the best choice. They use metadata standards your community expects, making your data findable by researchers in your field. Search [re3data](https://www.re3data.org/) to find repositories for your domain.

**General-purpose repositories** like [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/), [Figshare](https://figshare.com/), or [OSF](https://osf.io/) accept any data type. They provide DOIs and long-term preservation, but may lack the specialized metadata fields of discipline-specific options.

**Institutional repositories** may be required by your funder or institution. Check your grant terms and institutional policies.

Whichever you choose, ensure the repository provides a **DOI** (Digital Object Identifier) so your data can be cited and tracked. See the Identity & Credit panel for more on persistent identifiers.

::: {.orc-grid-tools}
::: {.orc-card-tool}
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><i class="bi bi-search"></i></div>

#### [re3data](https://www.re3data.org/)

Registry of research data repositories

<details>
<summary>Read more</summary>
Search by discipline to find repositories that accept your type of data and meet your requirements.
</details>
:::

::: {.orc-card-tool}
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><i class="bi bi-cloud-upload"></i></div>

#### [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/)

General-purpose repository for any research output

<details>
<summary>Read more</summary>
Free, open repository hosted by CERN. Accepts any file type, assigns DOIs, and integrates with GitHub.
</details>
:::
:::

#### 4.1.4. Data Licenses

A license tells others what they can do with your data. Without one, others cannot legally reuse your work, even if it is publicly available.

**CC0 (Public Domain)** places no restrictions. Anyone can use, modify, and redistribute without attribution. Recommended for factual data where maximum reuse is the goal.

**CC BY (Attribution)** requires users to credit you. A good default when you want recognition while enabling broad reuse.

**CC BY-NC (Non-Commercial)** adds a restriction against commercial use. Consider whether this limitation actually serves your goals.

For restricted-access data, a **Data Use Agreement** specifies conditions beyond a standard license: approved purposes, security requirements, publication terms, and data destruction timelines.

::: {.orc-grid-tools}
::: {.orc-card-tool}
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" alt="LMU OSC"></div>

#### [Choose a License (Tutorial)](https://lmu-osc.github.io/code-publishing/choose_license.html)

Guidance on selecting the right license

<details>
<summary>Read more</summary>
Covers licenses for data, code, and other research outputs with decision flowcharts.
</details>
:::
:::

:::

</details>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-code-square"></i> 

### Code Publishing {#sec-code-publishing}

</summary>

<!-- intro text here -->

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### 4.2.1. Preparing Your Repo

<!-- readme for external users, clean history, remove sensitive data -->

#### 4.2.2. Simulated & Synthetic Data

<!-- data to run: real/sim/synth -->

#### 4.2.3. Code Licenses

<!-- licence (link to code-publishing tutorial) -->

#### 4.2.4. Archiving & DOIs

<!-- zenodo workflow, DOI, link to https://lmu-osc.github.io/code-publishing/ -->

:::

</details>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-journal-arrow-up"></i> 

### Open Access Articles {#sec-open-access-articles}

</summary>

<!-- intro text here -->

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Your Rights

<!-- what publishers allow: openpolicyfinder -->

#### Green OA

<!-- preprint/postprint workflow figure, institutional repos, embargo periods -->

#### Gold OA

<!-- APCs, LMU agreements -->

:::

</details>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-person-badge"></i> 

### Identity & Credit {#sec-identity-credit}

</summary>

<!-- intro text here -->

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### 4.4.1. ORCID

<!-- ORCID, ROR IDs -->

####  4.4.2. CRediT

<!-- CRediT taxonomy, contributorship -->

####  4.4.3. Connecting Your Work

<!-- connect prereg → data → code → paper -->


:::

</details>




::: {.orc-gradient-box}

## <i class="bi bi-card-checklist"></i> Preservation Checklist {.unnumbered}

**Before Publication:**

<div class="orc-grid-2col">

<div>

- [ ] Data deposited in appropriate repository
- [ ] Metadata complete and discoverable
- [ ] Code published with documentation
- [ ] DOIs obtained for data and code
</div>

<div>

- [ ] All authors have ORCIDs linked
- [ ] Licenses applied (CC0/CC BY, MIT/Apache)
- [ ] Sensitive data removed and verified
- [ ] Ethics approval for sharing (if required)
</div>

</div>

**At Submission:**

<div class="orc-grid-2col">

<div>

- [ ] Data Availability Statement with DOIs
- [ ] Code availability materials linked
- [ ] Open access option selected
</div>

<div>

- [ ] ORCID, ROR, funder IDs included
- [ ] Author contributions (CRediT) specified
- [ ] Preprint posted (if applicable)
</div>

</div>

**After Acceptance:**

- [ ] Postprint uploaded to institutional repository
- [ ] Preprint updated with link to published version
- [ ] Repository READMEs updated with citation
- [ ] Co-authors receive final citation and PDF

**Long-term:**

- [ ] Verify DOIs resolve correctly
- [ ] Respond to data/code access requests
- [ ] Monitor citations and usage metrics

:::
